_metadata.description: Inverted Bloom's for the Age of AI — assessment-design framework that scaffolds students DOWN the agency scale (from AI-dependent Create toward student-owned Remember). Productive friction, ai_agency audit tag, and the post-creation learning journey.
_metadata.runtime_strategy_rationale: Body MD ~7,449 chars / 4 ≈ 1,862 tokens — within the 'embed' band per runtime_strategy_rules. Override to read_at_runtime per named_override_patterns.selective_load: canvas_course_expert loads 2-3 of ~12 knowledge files per audit task. Selective-load defeats embed-cache benefits.

scope: Assessment-design framework for the AI age — audit whether assessments produce genuine student learning or inadvertently accept AI-generated work as a substitute. Boundaries: does not cover CLO well-formedness (see outcomes_quality) or Bloom verb classification (see taxonomy_explorer); layers on top of those, not replaces them.

facts.definition: Traditional Bloom's (Remember → Understand → Apply → Analyze → Evaluate → Create) assumed students who produced sophisticated work had internalized the lower levels to get there. AI breaks that assumption: students now access tools that generate Create-level output on demand before they've built understanding, analysis, or memory. The output can look polished while the learning didn't happen.
citations: Kassorla, M. Inverted Bloom's for the Age of AI. Substack.

facts.definition: Six levels from maximum AI influence to minimal: Create (Lvl 1, submits AI-generated artifact with minimal engagement) → Evaluate (Lvl 2, judges AI output using AI-supplied criteria) → Analyze (Lvl 3, breaks down AI feedback superficially) → Apply (Lvl 4, implements insights with personal initiative) → Understand (Lvl 5, makes connections through reflection and iteration) → Remember (Lvl 6, internalizes knowledge independently). Goal of assessment design: scaffold students DOWN the agency scale toward student-owned understanding.

The cognitive struggle that generates authentic learning. AI bypasses this friction by default; assessment design must deliberately reintroduce it. Examples: revision cycles with instructor/peer feedback; staged drafts with required justification for changes; oral defense or live explanation; process documentation (annotated bibliography, design journal, revision history); failure states that must be diagnosed and corrected. Without productive friction, a polished submission is evidence of AI use, not learning.

Traditional outcome states what students will produce. AI-age outcomes add an ownership clause specifying how students will OWN the production. Example: 'Students will design a marketing campaign ... and explain, in a recorded debrief, why each design decision was made and what alternatives were rejected.' The added clause is not busywork — it is the only evidence the student traveled through Understand and Apply before arriving at Create.

Rubric evaluates only the final artifact (no criteria for process, reasoning, or iteration); no staged submission (no draft, outline, checkpoint); no peer review, reflection prompt, or oral component; CLO could be satisfied by submitting AI-generated response unchanged; text-only products with no embedded rationale.
citations: This knowledge file's Canvas Audit Indicators

facts.definition: At least one stage before final (draft or outline required); rubric includes at least one process criterion ('sources evaluated', 'revision explained'); reflection prompt exists but is separate, low-weight; peer feedback is optional or ungraded.
citations: This knowledge file's Canvas Audit Indicators

facts.definition: Rubric explicitly scores reasoning and decision-making, not just the artifact; multiple checkpoints with required justification for changes; oral defense, screenshare, or live explanation is part of the grade; process documentation graded equally or heavier than artifact; deliberate failure states or revision cycles; assignment-specific AI policy tied to rubric criteria.
citations: This knowledge file's Canvas Audit Indicators

facts.definition: For each graded assessment: (1) Could a student submit by pasting AI output and meet the rubric? Yes → ai_dependent. (2) Does the rubric evaluate how the student thought, not just what they submitted? No → ai_dependent or scaffolded at best. (3) Is there productive friction — a stage where failure is possible and must be corrected? Yes → candidate for student_owned.
citations: This knowledge file's Practical Test section

facts.definition: Value enum: ai_dependent | scaffolded | student_owned. Pairs with outcomes_quality (ownership-clause framing starts at outcome level), designer_thinking (backward design includes friction), cognitive_load_theory (germane load is precisely what AI bypasses; student_owned maximizes germane load).
citations: This knowledge file's Audit tag section

sources: Kassorla, M. Inverted Bloom's for the Age of AI. Substack. https://michellekassorla.substack.com/p/inverted-blooms-for-the-age-of-ai

pre_run_checklist: Each entry in consumed_by[] resolves to an existing agent spec
post_run_checklist: knowledge/inverted_blooms_knowledge.json exists with shape=reference and facts[] present
